module.title: Mixing Essentials
module.description: Build a clean, balanced mix from the ground up — gain staging, EQ, compression, stereo width, send/return routing, and professional referencing.

lessons.title: Gain Staging: Getting Levels Right
lessons.body:
Before you touch a single EQ or compressor, your levels need to be set correctly. Gain staging is the practice of managing signal level at every point in the chain so that nothing clips, nothing gets buried, and every plugin receives a clean, consistent input. It is the unglamorous foundation that separates a mix that translates everywhere from one that sounds fine on your laptop but falls apart on speakers.

## The Signal Chain in FL Studio

Every sound in FL Studio travels the same path:

**Instrument channel volume → Mixer track fader → Mixer track insert plugins → Master mixer track**

Each stage has its own gain control, and each stage is an opportunity to accidentally clip or crush the signal before it reaches the next one.

## Your Target: Headroom

The goal is to keep individual tracks peaking around **−18 to −12 dBFS** and your master bus peaking no higher than **−6 dBFS** before any limiter on the master. This headroom gives your plugins — especially compressors and saturators — the dynamic range they need to work cleanly.

| Stage | Target Peak | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Individual tracks | −18 to −12 dBFS | Plugin headroom, clean transients |
| Subgroups (drums bus, etc.) | −12 to −6 dBFS | Balanced blend before master |
| Master (pre-limiter) | −6 dBFS | Room for limiter to work without squashing |

## Where to Set Gain

FL Studio gives you three gain controls per sound. Use them in the right order:

**1. Instrument channel volume knob** (the knob at the top of each channel in the Channel Rack): Set this first. A full synth patch or a loud drum sample might already be hitting 0 dBFS at default. Turn it down until the mixer peak meter for that track reads around −18 dBFS on loud hits.

**2. Mixer track fader** (the large vertical fader in the Mixer): Leave this at 100% (unity, shown in green) while you set relative levels. Once everything is properly staged, use faders for blend decisions.

**3. Insert plugin input/output gain**: Many plugins — compressors, saturators — have their own input and output gain knobs. Match their output to their input so you are hearing the effect of the plugin, not a volume change.

## Reading the Meters

The peak meter on each mixer track shows instantaneous and peak-hold levels:

- **Green (−∞ to −6 dBFS)**: Good — signal is present and healthy
- **Yellow (−6 to −3 dBFS)**: Caution — approaching the ceiling
- **Red (above −3 dBFS / clipping indicator)**: Clip — you need to reduce gain upstream

When a red clip indicator appears at the top of a meter, it stays lit until you click it to reset. It is telling you that clipping occurred at some point — find and fix the source before mixing further.

## A Practical Gain-Staging Workflow

1. **Mute everything** except your kick drum
2. Play the loop and adjust the kick's channel volume until the mixer track peaks around −18 dBFS
3. Unmute the snare, repeat
4. Work through every drum element, then hi-hats, percussion
5. Add bass, melody, and chords — adjust each to sit in the same ballpark
6. Check your master: it should be peaking well below −6 dBFS with no red
7. From here, use **faders** to blend — not the channel volume knobs

## Why Lower Is Better

Two reasons beginners resist this:

**Ear fatigue**: Louder mixes feel better in the moment. After 30 minutes your ears compensate and you start making bad decisions — boosting highs that are already sharp, adding low end that is already muddy. Mix quiet, check loud.

**Plugin behaviour**: Compressors, distortion, and saturation plugins all respond differently depending on input level. If your gain staging is inconsistent, the same compressor setting sounds different on every track. Consistent input levels mean consistent, predictable plugin behaviour.
keyTakeaways: Target −18 to −12 dBFS on individual tracks and −6 dBFS on the master before any limiter — headroom makes every plugin behave predictably.
Use the instrument channel volume knob to set initial gain, keep mixer faders at unity (100%), and check that no red clip indicators appear before you start EQ or compression.
Mixing at lower monitor levels reduces ear fatigue and prevents overcorrecting — your ears adapt to loudness within minutes.
lessons.tryThis: Open a project with at least four tracks routed to the mixer. Click any red clip indicators to reset them, then play the loop. For each track that peaks above −12 dBFS, reduce the instrument channel volume until it sits in the −18 to −12 dBFS range. Once all tracks are staged, check the master — it should peak well below −6 dBFS. Note how the mix sounds relative to before: it will feel quieter but cleaner.

lessons.title: EQ Basics with Parametric EQ 2
lessons.body:
Every sound occupies frequency space. When you stack drums, bass, chords, and melody on top of each other, they compete for the same sonic real estate. EQ — equalization — is the tool you use to carve space for each element so the mix can be heard clearly at any volume.

FL Studio's **Parametric EQ 2** is one of the best free EQs available. It ships with every version of FL Studio and does everything a professional mix engineer needs.

## Opening Parametric EQ 2

In the Mixer, click an empty insert slot on any track and select **Parametric EQ 2**. The plugin opens a frequency display showing the current EQ curve overlaid on a spectrum analyser.

## The Interface

Parametric EQ 2 has **7 bands**, each configurable as one of several types:

| Band Type | Shape | Use For |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Pass** | Rolls off above cutoff | Remove high-frequency hiss, air |
| **High Pass** | Rolls off below cutoff | Remove low-end rumble |
| **Low Shelf** | Boosts/cuts all lows | Add or remove overall warmth |
| **High Shelf** | Boosts/cuts all highs | Add or remove overall air/brightness |
| **Peak (Bell)** | Boost or cut around a centre frequency | Surgical cuts, targeted boosts |
| **Notch** | Deep, narrow cut | Remove a specific resonant frequency |
| **Band Pass** | Passes only the band, cuts above and below | Special effect or diagnostic |

Each band has three parameters:
- **Frequency** (Hz): where the EQ is applied
- **Gain** (dB): how much to boost (+) or cut (−)
- **Q (bandwidth)**: how wide or narrow the effect is. High Q = narrow surgical cut. Low Q = wide, musical boost.

## The Core Principle: Subtractive First

Cuts almost always sound more natural than boosts. The professional approach:

1. **Cut what hurts** — remove problematic frequencies first
2. **Boost what helps** — add character only after removing mud and harshness
3. **Use your ears, not your eyes** — a flat-looking curve can sound terrible; a jagged-looking curve can sound great

## The High-Pass Filter: Your First Move on Almost Everything

Apply a **High Pass filter** to every non-bass track in your mix. Low-frequency energy below 80–100 Hz is usually inaudible on most non-bass instruments but takes up headroom and muddies the low end.

- Click the leftmost band, right-click and set to **High Pass**
- Drag the frequency to around 80 Hz for most instruments
- 120–180 Hz for brighter instruments (hi-hats, synth pads, vocals)
- Leave kick and bass untouched — they *need* that low end

## Finding Problem Frequencies: The Sweep Technique

When a sound has an annoying resonance or harshness you cannot identify by ear alone:

1. Add a **Peak** band with a high Q (narrow) and a large boost (+12 dB)
2. Slowly drag the frequency across the problem area
3. When the annoying quality becomes exaggerated, you have found it
4. Switch the gain from a boost to a **cut** (−4 to −8 dB) and lower the Q to a moderate width
5. You have just removed a resonance without guessing

## Common EQ Moves by Instrument

**Kick drum**
- High pass around 30–40 Hz (sub rumble, not the fundamental)
- Boost 60–80 Hz for sub punch (low Q, gentle +2 to +4 dB)
- Cut 250–350 Hz for boominess/mud
- Boost 3–5 kHz for click and attack (helps cut through on small speakers)

**Snare**
- High pass around 100 Hz
- Boost 180–250 Hz for body and fatness
- Cut 400–600 Hz if it sounds boxy
- Boost 5–10 kHz for snap and crack

**Bass / 808**
- No high pass (you need those lows)
- Cut 200–400 Hz if the bass feels muddy or boomy
- A gentle boost at 700 Hz–1 kHz helps bass translate on small speakers

**Synth leads and melodies**
- High pass 100–150 Hz
- Cut the 200–400 Hz range if the sound feels thick or boxy
- Boost 3–8 kHz carefully for presence and definition

**Pads and chords**
- High pass aggressively: 200–300 Hz. Pads rarely need any low end.
- Low pass around 10–12 kHz to remove the high-frequency wash that clutters the top end

## Using the Spectrum Analyser

Right-click the display area in Parametric EQ 2 and enable the analyser. The real-time spectrum shows you which frequencies are loudest in the signal. Use it to:

- Confirm your high-pass is working (the left side should drop off cleanly)
- Spot unexpected resonances (a spike that sticks up above its neighbours)
- Check that cuts are actually removing what you intended

**Important**: The analyser is a diagnostic tool, not a target. Do not try to flatten the spectrum — music is not supposed to be flat.
keyTakeaways: Apply a high-pass filter to every non-bass track first — removing inaudible sub-rumble cleans up the low end of the whole mix without touching the bass.
Cut before you boost: use the sweep technique (narrow high-Q boost, drag slowly) to locate problem resonances, then switch to a moderate cut at the same frequency.
EQ decisions are always in service of the mix, not the solo: a sound that looks 'wrong' on the analyser may sit perfectly in context, and vice versa.
lessons.tryThis: Pick a chord pad or synth in your mix and open Parametric EQ 2 on its mixer track. First, set band 1 to High Pass and drag the frequency to 250 Hz — notice how the bottom of the sound disappears but the mix actually gets cleaner. Now use the sweep technique: add a narrow peak band with +12 dB gain and drag it slowly between 200 and 600 Hz until the most 'boxy' or 'honky' quality is exaggerated. Flip the gain to −6 dB. Toggle the EQ in and out (power button) and listen to how the sound sits better in the full mix.

lessons.title: Compression with Fruity Limiter
lessons.body:
Compression is the most misunderstood tool in mixing. Used badly, it kills energy and makes everything sound flat. Used well, it glues elements together, controls transients that would otherwise poke out, and makes a mix feel punchy and consistent. This lesson focuses on **Fruity Limiter** — FL Studio's built-in compressor and limiter — which does everything you need to learn compression properly.

## What Compression Actually Does

A compressor watches the incoming signal level. When that level exceeds a set **threshold**, the compressor turns it down by a ratio you specify. The result is a smaller gap between the loudest and quietest moments — a reduced **dynamic range**.

Why would you want that? Two main reasons:

1. **Tame transients**: A snare or pluck has a sharp attack spike that jumps above everything else. Compression catches that spike and pulls it back, making the sound sit more evenly in the mix.
2. **Add sustain and glue**: After compressing the loud peak down, you can turn the whole signal back up (make-up gain). Now the quiet tail is louder relative to the peak — the sound feels thicker and longer.

## Opening Fruity Limiter in Compressor Mode

Add Fruity Limiter to an insert slot on your mixer track. By default it shows the **LIMIT** tab. Click the **COMP** tab to switch to compressor mode — that is where all the useful controls are.

## The Five Controls

**Threshold** — the level at which compression starts. Signals below the threshold pass through untouched. Signals above it get turned down. Set lower = more compression. Start around −18 dB and adjust to taste.

**Ratio** — how much the signal above the threshold is reduced. A ratio of 4:1 means for every 4 dB the signal goes above the threshold, only 1 dB comes out. Common ratios:

| Ratio | Character | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 2:1 – 3:1 | Gentle, transparent | Buses, vocals, subtle glue |
| 4:1 – 6:1 | Moderate | Drums, bass, most sources |
| 8:1 – 10:1 | Heavy, pumping | 808s, aggressive snare, effect |
| 20:1+ | Limiting (brick wall) | Peak control, master bus |

**Attack** — how quickly the compressor responds after the signal crosses the threshold. Measured in milliseconds.
- **Fast attack (1–10 ms)**: Catches transients immediately — tightens a snapping snare, removes the click from a bass note.
- **Slow attack (30–100 ms)**: Lets the transient through before clamping — preserves punch and crack on a kick.

**Release** — how quickly the compressor stops compressing after the signal drops below the threshold.
- **Fast release (50–150 ms)**: The compressor recovers quickly — can cause pumping at lower settings.
- **Slow release (300–600 ms)**: Smoother, more natural — good starting point for most sources.
- **Musical release trick**: set release so the compressor recovers in time with the tempo. At 120 BPM, one beat is 500 ms — a release around 400–500 ms will breathe with the track.

**Gain (Make-up Gain)** — after compressing, the signal is quieter. Make-up gain brings the output back up to match the input level. The gain reduction meter (the bar that drops when compression is active) shows you how much you are compressing — use make-up gain to compensate for the same amount.

## Gain Reduction: How Much Is Right?

Watch the gain reduction meter — the downward bar on the compressor. Aim for:
- **2–4 dB reduction**: Transparent, subtle control. Usually the right amount for melodic elements.
- **4–8 dB reduction**: Obvious compression — good for snares, 808s, punchy bass.
- **8+ dB reduction**: Heavy effect — pumping, breath, intentional character.

If you cannot hear the compressor in bypass, you are probably not compressing enough or not using make-up gain. A/B properly: match levels first, then toggle.

## The LIMIT Tab: Brick Wall Ceiling

The **LIMIT** tab turns Fruity Limiter into a true limiter — a compressor with an effectively infinite ratio. Nothing gets through above the ceiling.

Use limiting on the **master bus** to:
- Prevent digital clipping (set ceiling to −0.3 dBFS, never 0)
- Control the final loudness of the track
- Give the mix a competitive level without distorting

Do not use heavy limiting to compensate for a mix that is already too loud — fix the mix first, then limit last.

## Practical Starting Points by Instrument

**Kick drum**: Slow attack (40–60 ms) to preserve transient punch. Moderate ratio (4:1). Release around 100–200 ms. 3–5 dB reduction.

**Snare**: Faster attack (5–15 ms) to catch the snap. Ratio 4:1–6:1. Short release (80–150 ms). 4–6 dB reduction.

**808 / bass**: Slow attack (20–40 ms). Ratio 3:1–6:1. Medium release (200–400 ms). Keeps the sub consistent without killing the slide.

**Melody / lead synth**: Very gentle. Ratio 2:1–3:1. Medium-slow attack and release. 2–3 dB reduction. Goal: evening out note-to-note level differences, not squashing.

**Drum bus (all drums together)**: Low ratio (2:1), moderate threshold, slow attack and release. 1–3 dB reduction. Glues the kit without pumping.
keyTakeaways: Compression reduces the gap between loud and quiet — attack controls whether transients pass through first (slow) or get caught immediately (fast), and release controls how naturally the compressor recovers.
Aim for 2–6 dB of gain reduction on most tracks, then use make-up gain to restore the perceived level — that way you can A/B fairly and hear what the compressor is actually doing.
Use the LIMIT tab on your master bus with a ceiling of −0.3 dBFS to prevent clipping — but fix gain staging and mix balance first, because limiting cannot rescue a loud, muddy mix.
lessons.tryThis: Open Fruity Limiter on your snare track and click the COMP tab. Set ratio to 4:1 and threshold to −20 dB. Play your loop and watch the gain reduction meter — it should be moving 4–6 dB on every hit. Now try attack at 5 ms vs 60 ms: notice how slow attack preserves the initial crack of the snare while fast attack smooths it out. Add make-up gain to match the bypassed level, then toggle the compressor on and off — the snare should sit more consistently in the mix with compression active.

lessons.title: Panning and Stereo Width
lessons.body:
A flat mix with every element panned centre sounds narrow, lacks depth, and makes individual sounds harder to distinguish. Panning and stereo width create the left-right dimension of your mix — the spatial field that makes a track feel wide, open, and professional.

## The Stereo Field

Think of the stereo field as a horizontal stage, from hard left (−100) to centre (0) to hard right (+100). The listener hears a three-dimensional space even though it is two channels of audio.

Centre is the most powerful position — anything panned dead centre commands the most attention and perceived loudness. Use centre strategically for the most important elements.

## What Stays in the Centre

Some elements must stay centred for the mix to translate correctly across different playback systems — mono phones, car stereos, club PA systems:

- Kick drum
- Bass / 808 (all sub energy should be mono)
- Lead vocal or main melody
- Master/full-mix elements

Low frequencies below about 150 Hz are non-directional — the human ear cannot perceive their stereo position. Panning bass hard to one side does not create a spatial effect; it just creates an unbalanced mix.

## What Gets Panned

Everything else can and should spread across the field:

| Element | Suggested Pan | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Snare | Slight left or centre | Never hard — it loses punch |
| Hi-hats (open/closed) | Opposite sides, moderate | Mimics a live drum kit |
| Percussion hits | Scattered L/R | Adds width without mud |
| Chord pads | Wide left and right | Creates the sense of space |
| Backing synths | Alternate L/R | L version and R version of same sound |
| Counter-melody | Opposite side to lead | Keeps both audible |
| FX and risers | Full stereo | Let them breathe |

## Panning in FL Studio

The mixer track has a **pan knob** directly above the fader. Rotate left to pan left, right to pan right. You can also right-click and type an exact value.

For the Channel Rack, individual steps in the Step Sequencer have no per-step pan — pan is controlled at the mixer track level.

## Stereo Width Tools

Panning a mono sound moves it to one side. **Stereo width** is different — it controls how wide a stereo signal spreads from left to right.

### Fruity Stereo Enhancer
Add this to an insert slot. The **Pan** knob sets left-right position. The **Width** knob (−1 to +1 on the Stereo Separation dial) controls the stereo spread:
- **Width = 0**: Mono — both channels identical
- **Width = 1 (default)**: Normal stereo
- **Width > 1**: Enhanced width — sounds wider than a normal stereo recording
- **Width = −1**: Stereo reversed (L and R channels swapped)

Use increased width on pads and chords. Use reduced width or mono on bass elements.

### The Haas Effect (Manual Width)
Duplicate a sound to two mixer tracks. Pan one hard left, pan the other hard right. Delay the right channel by 20–35 ms (using **Fruity Delay 3** or a sample delay plugin with 0 feedback). The slight timing difference creates a sense of width that sounds very natural. Be careful — this can cause phase issues in mono playback.

### Mid-Side Awareness
When you widen a stereo signal, you are amplifying the **Side** channel (L−R information). Too much width can cause the mix to collapse or phase-cancel when played back in mono. Always check your wide mix in mono:

In the Mixer master track, add **Fruity Stereo Enhancer** with Width set to 0 — this collapses everything to mono. If elements disappear or the mix sounds thin, reduce the width on those elements.

## A Practical Panning Workflow

1. Start with everything centred
2. Pan drums realistically (kick centre, hi-hats slightly offset)
3. Pan chord pads hard left and right (use duplicate tracks or stereo width)
4. Pan any secondary melody opposite to the lead
5. Keep bass, kick, and lead dead centre
6. Check the mix in mono — nothing important should disappear
7. Make small adjustments — most elements should not be panned past ±50 unless the track calls for it

## Panning vs. Frequency Separation

Panning is most effective when combined with EQ. Two elements occupying the same frequency range and both panned to the same side will still compete. The most musical mixes use both dimensions together: pan elements apart *and* give them distinct frequency zones.
keyTakeaways: Keep kick, bass, and lead melody centred — low frequencies are non-directional and off-centre sub energy creates an unbalanced mix on mono systems.
Check your mix in mono by collapsing Fruity Stereo Enhancer to Width 0 on the master — if elements disappear, excessive stereo width is causing phase cancellation.
Panning is most effective combined with EQ: two sounds competing at the same frequency range will still clash even if panned apart — give each element its own frequency zone and spatial position.
lessons.tryThis: In your mix, pan your chord pads or background synths: send the left instance to −60 and the right to +60. Now add Fruity Stereo Enhancer to each pad track and push the width slightly past 1. Play the full mix and notice how the centre clears up — kick and lead feel more prominent without changing their volume. Then add Fruity Stereo Enhancer to the master track, set Width to 0 to force mono, and listen. If the pads become thin or hollow, pull their width back until they survive mono playback.
